Venus rotates backwards compared to most planets. Nobody's told it that's unusual.
A day on Venus outlasts its year. Nobody's rushing.
Jupiter has at least 95 moons. Earth has one and manages fine.
Saturn would float in a big enough bathtub. Water density, not an invitation to try it.
Mars has the largest volcano in the solar system, three times Everest's height, retired.
Mercury has no real atmosphere, so its days swing 800 degrees from noon to night.
Neptune's winds top 1,200 mph. Nobody up there has ever complained, on record.
Uranus rotates on its side. Something hit it very hard, a long time ago.
A year on Neptune takes 165 Earth years. It's had exactly one since discovery in 1846.
Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm bigger than Earth. It's raged for centuries.
Mars's two moons, Phobos and Deimos, are so small they barely count as spherical.
Venus's surface is hot enough to melt lead. The clouds up top are mostly sulfuric acid.
Saturn's rings are mostly ice, some chunks house-sized, some just dust.
Mercury has ice in permanently shadowed craters, despite being closest to the Sun.
Jupiter outweighs every other planet in the solar system combined, twice over.
A year on Mercury is shorter than a Mercury day. It orbits faster than it spins.
Uranus was almost named after King George III: Herschel called it 'Georgium Sidus.'
Saturn's moon Titan has lakes and rivers, of liquid methane, not water.
Mars's dust storms can occasionally cover the entire planet for weeks.
Venus has no moons and barely any tilt. It skipped two common planetary habits.
Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the solar system.
Neptune was predicted by math before anyone actually saw it through a telescope.
Saturn's density is low enough that, hypothetically, the planet would float on water.
Mercury's orbit was the first confirmed test of Einstein's general relativity, in 1915.
Jupiter's moon Europa likely holds more water than all of Earth's oceans, under its ice.
Uranus has the coldest planetary atmosphere in the solar system, colder than Neptune's.
Mars's sunsets appear blue, the opposite of Earth's, thanks to fine atmospheric dust.
Venus spins so slowly its day is longer than its year, by about 18 Earth days.
Saturn's moon Enceladus shoots water-ice geysers hundreds of miles into space.
Jupiter has a faint ring system too. It's just outshone by the obvious one at Saturn.
Mercury has essentially no axial tilt, so it has no real seasons to speak of.
Neptune's moon Triton orbits backwards, a sign it's a captured object, not an original.
Mars's atmosphere is less than 1% as thick as Earth's. Bring your own air, hypothetically.
Saturn takes about 29 Earth years to orbit the Sun once. Patience, at planetary scale.
Uranus has 13 known rings, found only in 1977, almost by accident during a stellar event.
The Moon drifts from Earth by about 1.5 inches a year. No rush expected.
The same side of the Moon always faces Earth. The far side got its first photo in 1959.
Moonlight is just borrowed sunlight, delayed by about 1.3 seconds getting here.
The Moon has quakes. Weaker than Earth's, but some last for hours.
A 'supermoon' looks about 14% bigger and 30% brighter, mostly an orbital timing trick.
The Moon has no atmosphere, so footprints up there aren't going anywhere soon.
Lunar days last about 29.5 Earth days, two weeks of daylight, then two of night.
The Moon likely formed from debris after a Mars-sized object hit early Earth.
Blue moons aren't blue. The name just means a second full moon in one month.
The Moon's gravity is about a sixth of Earth's. Jumping is cheap up there.
Total lunar eclipses turn the Moon reddish, not dark, as Earth's atmosphere bends light through.
The Moon has no wind or weather, so craters from millions of years ago just stay put.
Twelve people have walked on the Moon, all of them between 1969 and 1972.
The Moon is tidally locked to Earth, which is why it always shows the same face.
Moon dust is surprisingly abrasive. It's part of why Apollo suits wore out fast.
The Moon has a very thin exosphere, technically an atmosphere, just barely.
A 'harvest moon' is just the full moon nearest the autumn equinox. Predates streetlights.
The Moon's far side has far fewer dark 'seas' than the near side. No one's fully sure why.
Temperatures on the Moon swing from about 250°F in daylight to -208°F at night.
The Moon is the fifth-largest moon in the solar system, and by far the biggest relative to its planet.
Moonquakes were first measured by seismometers Apollo astronauts left behind.
The Moon's gravity drives most of Earth's tides. The Sun helps, more quietly.
There's no 'dark side' of the Moon, just a far side. It gets the same sunlight as the near one.
The first close-up photos of the Moon's far side came from a Soviet probe in 1959.
The Moon is slowly stealing Earth's rotational energy, which is lengthening our days too.
A lunar 'libration' effect lets viewers on Earth see slightly more than half the Moon over time.
The Moon has water ice in permanently shadowed polar craters, confirmed by NASA in 2009.
Full moons in winter tend to ride higher in the sky, mirroring the low summer Sun.
The Moon's crust is thicker on the far side, one theory for why it has fewer visible 'seas.'
Apollo 11's descent computer had less processing power than a modern calculator.
The nearest star to the Sun, Proxima Centauri, is about 4.2 light-years away. Still too far.
Most stars you see at night are larger and hotter than the Sun, which is solidly average.
Polaris, the North Star, isn't the brightest star in the sky. Just conveniently placed.
Starlight you're seeing tonight left those stars years, centuries, sometimes millennia ago.
The Big Dipper isn't a constellation. It's an asterism, a shape within Ursa Major.
Betelgeuse is so large that, in our solar system, it would swallow Jupiter's orbit.
The Milky Way holds an estimated 100 to 400 billion stars. Precision optional at that scale.
A 'shooting star' is dust or rock burning up in the atmosphere. No star involved.
Sirius is the brightest star in the night sky, and not even in the top twenty for size.
Most stars in the sky belong to multiple-star systems. Lone stars like the Sun are less common.
The Andromeda Galaxy is on a slow collision course with the Milky Way, due in 4 billion years.
Stars twinkle because of atmospheric turbulence, not anything happening at the star itself.
Planets don't twinkle the way stars do. Their light comes from a small disc, not a point.
A star's color reveals its temperature: blue-white is hottest, red is coolest.
A neutron star can pack more mass than the Sun into a sphere the size of a city.
Some stars you see may have already died. The light's still arriving; the star may not be.
The Pleiades star cluster is visible to the naked eye as a small, tight group of actual siblings.
A light-year measures distance, not time, about 5.9 trillion miles, if it comes up.
The darkest skies on Earth can show around 2,500 stars to the naked eye at once.
Constellations are optical illusions of alignment. The stars in one aren't actually near each other.
The Sun is a star. Calling it anything else is technically incorrect, if unremarkable.
Globular clusters can hold hundreds of thousands of stars in a ball a few dozen light-years wide.
The Southern Cross isn't visible from most of the Northern Hemisphere, a genuinely regional sight.
Red giants are stars running low on hydrogen, swelling up before a quieter ending.
A supernova can briefly outshine its entire host galaxy. Briefly is doing some work there.
The Summer Triangle isn't a constellation. It's three bright stars from three different ones.
Some binary stars orbit each other in mere hours. Others take thousands of years per lap.
The zodiac constellations are just the ones the Sun appears to pass through. Plain geometry.
White dwarfs are Sun-like stars' eventual remains: Earth-sized, and unbelievably dense.
The night sky looks different from the Southern Hemisphere. Different stars, no Big Dipper.
Black holes don't suck things in from a distance any more than any other mass would.
The closest known black hole is over 1,500 light-years away. Comfortably theoretical.
A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about a billion tons on Earth.
The Milky Way's disc is about 100,000 light-years across. Not a weekend trip.
Some stars in Orion's Belt aren't actually close to each other, just aligned from here.
The ISS orbits Earth roughly every 90 minutes, sixteen sunrises a day for its crew.
The International Space Station has been continuously occupied since November 2000.
The ISS travels at about 17,500 mph, fast enough to cross the US in about 10 minutes.
Astronauts on the ISS see the sun rise and set 16 times in a single 24-hour day.
The ISS is roughly the size of a football field, including its solar arrays.
Sputnik, the first artificial satellite, orbited Earth for about three months in 1957.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space in 1961, orbiting Earth once.
The Apollo program landed twelve astronauts on the Moon across six missions.
Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is now the most distant human-made object from Earth.
The Hubble Space Telescope has been operating since 1990, well past its planned lifespan.
The ISS is a joint project among five space agencies representing 15 countries.
Laika, a stray dog, was the first animal to orbit Earth, aboard Sputnik 2 in 1957.
The Space Shuttle program flew 135 missions between 1981 and 2011.
Voyager 1 and 2 both carry a 'Golden Record' with sounds and images of Earth.
The first spacewalk was performed by Alexei Leonov in 1965, for about 12 minutes.
The ISS can be seen with the naked eye. It's the third-brightest object in the sky.
GPS satellites correct for both special and general relativity just to stay accurate.
The Mars rover Curiosity has run since 2012, well past its planned two-year mission.
The James Webb Space Telescope orbits not Earth, but a stable point beyond the Moon.
China's Tiangong space station has been continuously crewed since 2022.
The first commercial resupply mission docked with the ISS in 2012, a SpaceX Dragon capsule.
Apollo 13's crew returned safely after an oxygen tank explosion, using the lunar module as a lifeboat.
The ISS's solar arrays generate more power than most small towns' rooftop solar combined.
Astronauts aboard the ISS age very slightly slower than people on Earth, thanks to relativity.
The first satellite photo of Earth from orbit was taken in 1946, on a captured V-2 rocket.
The Kármán line, 62 miles up, is the informal boundary where 'space' officially begins.
Skylab, America's first space station, fell back to Earth in 1979, scattering debris over Australia.
The ISS has hosted astronauts from more than 20 different countries.
New Horizons took about nine and a half years to reach Pluto after its 2006 launch.
The Perseverance rover carries Ingenuity, the first aircraft to fly on Mars.
The ISS orbits about 250 miles up, closer than many international flights' cruising range.
Sally Ride became the first American woman in space in 1983.
The Soviet Venera program landed probes on Venus, briefly, before the heat destroyed them.
The Mir space station operated for 15 years, far beyond its original 5-year design life.
Cargo missions regularly restock the ISS with food, fuel, and the occasional care package.
Auroras happen when solar particles collide with Earth's atmosphere near the magnetic poles.
Aurora colors depend on altitude and gas: green from oxygen, red higher up, blue-purple from nitrogen.
The aurora borealis and aurora australis are mirror-image events at opposite poles.
A strong solar flare can push aurora visible far south of its usual polar range.
Aurora activity roughly follows the Sun's 11-year solar cycle, peaking and fading in turn.
The Kp index measures geomagnetic activity 0 to 9; higher means aurora reaches farther south.
Auroras have been recorded on other planets with magnetic fields, including Jupiter and Saturn.
The word 'aurora' comes from the Roman goddess of dawn. 'Borealis' just means 'northern.'
Auroras typically occur 60 to 250 miles up, well above where commercial jets fly.
A geomagnetic storm can disrupt satellites and power grids, not just produce a light show.
Some of the best aurora viewing spots are also some of the coldest inhabited places on Earth.
Aurora forecasts are genuine forecasts. Solar wind takes one to three days to arrive.
The 1859 Carrington Event was a solar storm strong enough to spark aurora near the equator.
Auroras can shimmer, pulse, or ripple. The shapes depend on the local magnetic field lines.
Indigenous cultures across the Arctic have their own long-standing names and stories for the aurora.
A truly dark sky matters as much for aurora viewing as the forecast itself.
Aurora displays can last minutes or persist for hours, depending on the solar wind's staying power.
The solar wind that fuels aurora travels from the Sun to Earth at 400 to 800 km/s.
Aurora rarely appears the same way twice. No two nights of activity look quite identical.
Cameras often pick up faint aurora color the naked eye misses, especially with longer exposure.
The observable universe is about 93 billion light-years across, and still not the whole thing.
There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on every beach on Earth.
The universe is estimated to be about 13.8 billion years old.
Galaxies come in shapes: spiral, elliptical, and irregular. The Milky Way is a fairly ordinary spiral.
Most galaxies, including the Milky Way, have a supermassive black hole at their center.
Light from the edge of the observable universe has traveled since nearly the beginning of time.
Space isn't completely empty. Even the emptiest regions hold a few atoms per cubic meter.
The term 'light-year' was coined before anyone had measured one precisely.
Cosmic microwave background radiation is the afterglow of the early universe, still detectable.
There's no sound in space. No atmosphere means nothing to carry sound waves.
Earth's day is very slowly getting longer, thanks to the Moon's pull on our rotation.
The asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter is mostly empty space, despite how it's usually drawn.
Comets grow tails only when they get close enough to the Sun for ice to vaporize.
Meteor showers happen when Earth passes through debris left behind by a comet's old path.
The Perseid meteor shower peaks every August, courtesy of comet Swift-Tuttle's leftover trail.
Some exoplanets orbit two stars at once, actual binary sunsets, not just movie effects.
Over 5,000 exoplanets have been confirmed so far, with many more candidates awaiting confirmation.
The nearest known black hole is thousands of light-years away. No need to reroute anything.
Space telescopes skip atmospheric distortion entirely, which is why their images look so sharp.
The term 'planet' wasn't formally defined until 2006, the same year Pluto lost the title.
Pluto has five known moons; its largest, Charon, is over half Pluto's own diameter.
A 'blood moon' is just a fully eclipsed moon, tinted red by sunlight bending through Earth's atmosphere.
Solar eclipses happen because the Sun and Moon appear almost exactly the same size from Earth.
That size match between the Sun and Moon is a coincidence of current cosmic timing, not permanent.
Earth's atmosphere blocks most cosmic rays, quietly, all day, every day.
A geostationary satellite orbits at just the altitude needed to stay above one fixed point.
The gap in Saturn's rings, the Cassini Division, is visible from Earth with a decent telescope.
Halley's Comet returns roughly every 76 years; it's next expected back around 2061.
A 'nebula' just means a cloud of gas and dust. Some are stellar nurseries, others stellar remains.
The Sun accounts for more than 99.8% of the solar system's total mass.
Solar wind is a constant stream of charged particles from the Sun, not just an occasional event.
The zodiacal light, a faint glow after dusk, comes from sunlight scattering off interplanetary dust.
Earth's magnetic field, generated deep in its core, shields us from most solar radiation.
A 'planet parade,' several planets visible together, is just orbital alignment, not omen.
The gas giants have no solid surface to land on, just increasingly dense atmosphere all the way down.
Space agencies track thousands of pieces of orbital debris to avoid collisions with satellites.
The James Webb telescope observes mostly in infrared, letting it see through cosmic dust clouds.
A 'dark sky preserve' is a place formally protected from light pollution for stargazing.
The zodiac's thirteenth constellation, Ophiuchus, rarely gets mentioned outside of trivia.
Interstellar space begins where the Sun's solar wind stops pushing back against the galaxy's own particles.
Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is the only moon in the solar system with a substantial atmosphere.
Mercury and Venus are the only two planets in our solar system without any moons.
The 'Great Attractor' is a gravitational anomaly pulling galaxies, including ours, toward it.
A single galaxy can hold star systems ranging from newly formed to nearly as old as the universe.
The Drake Equation estimates how many communicating civilizations might exist. Every term's a guess.
The Sun loses about 4 million tons of mass every second, converted straight into light and heat.
Saturn's moon Mimas looks uncannily like the Death Star, entirely by coincidence of geology.
The record for consecutive days in space by one person is over a year, aboard Mir and the ISS.
A 'blue supermoon' combines two separate rarities and still isn't actually blue.
Footprints on the Moon are expected to last millions of years with no wind to erode them.
Ceres, in the asteroid belt, is large enough to be classified as a dwarf planet.
Uranus's moons are named after Shakespeare and Pope characters, not mythology like most others.
The fastest human-made object, the Parker Solar Probe, has grazed the Sun's outer atmosphere.
Earth is the only planet in the solar system not named after a Greek or Roman deity.
The 'morning star' and 'evening star' are both just Venus, depending on the time of year.
A comet's tail always points away from the Sun, regardless of which way the comet is traveling.
The Voyager probes are expected to keep transmitting, faintly, into the 2030s before going quiet.
Mars's two moons are thought to be captured asteroids, not formation debris like Earth's Moon.
The coldest recorded temperature in the solar system was measured on Neptune's moon Triton.
A planetary 'occultation' is just one body passing directly in front of another, from here.
